title: Associative property of multiplication
description: Practice changing the grouping of factors in multiplication problems and see how it affects the product.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kaley solved $\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times\maroonE{2}$ using the equations below.

$\begin{align}
\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2} &=(\redE{10}\times\purpleE{5}) \times\maroonE{2}\\\\ 
&=50 \times\maroonE{2}\\\\\  
&=100
\end{align}$

**Use the equations below to solve $\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2}$ in a different way.**

||
-:|:-
$\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2}$ | $=\redE{10}\times($ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]${}\times\maroonE{2})$  |
|$= \redE{10}\times {}$  [[☃ numeric-input 3]]|
|$= 100$|
hints: The parentheses tell us which two numbers to multiply first.

Kaley solved the equation by multiplying $(\redE{10}\times\purpleE{5})$ first, and then multiplying by $\maroonE{2}$.

$\begin{align}
\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2} &=(\redE{10}\times\purpleE{5}) \times\maroonE{2}\\\\
&=\blueE{50}\times\maroonE{2}\\\\
&=100
\end{align}$

In the new equation below, we want to multiply $(\purpleE{5}\times\maroonE{2})$ first, and then multiply by $\redE{10}$.

$\begin{align}
\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2} &= \redE{10}\times(\purpleE{5}\times\maroonE{2})\\\\
&=\redE{10}\times\goldE{10}\\\\ 
&=100
\end{align}$

Here are the completed equations:

$\begin{align}
\redE{10} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2} &= \redE{10}\times(\purpleE{5}\times\maroonE{2})\\\\
&=\redE{10}\times\goldE{10}\\\\ 
&=100
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Mayra solved $\redE{6} \times \purpleE{2} \times\maroonE{4}$ using the equations below.

$\begin{align}
\redE{6} \times \purpleE{2} \times \maroonE{4} &=(\redE{6}\times\purpleE{2}) \times\maroonE{4}\\\\ 
&=12 \times\maroonE{4}\\\\\  
&=48
\end{align}$

**Use the equations below to solve $\redE{6} \times \purpleE{2} \times \maroonE{4}$ in a different way.**

||
-:|:-
$\redE{6} \times \purpleE{2} \times \maroonE{4}$ | $=\redE{6}\times($ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]${}\times\maroonE{4})$  |
|$= \redE{6}\times {}$  [[☃ numeric-input 2]]|
|$= 48$|
hints: The parentheses tell us which two numbers to multiply first.

Mayra solved the equation by multiplying $(\redE{6}\times\purpleE{2})$ first, and then multiplying by $\maroonE{4}$.

$\begin{align}
\redE{6} \times \purpleE{2} \times \maroonE{4} &=(\redE{6}\times\purpleE{2}) \times\maroonE{4}\\\\
&=\blueE{12}\times\maroonE{4}\\\\
&=48
\end{align}$

In the new equation below, we want to multiply $(\purpleE{2}\times\maroonE{4})$ first, and then multiply by $\redE{6}$.

$\begin{align}
\redE{6} \times \purpleE{2} \times \maroonE{4} &= \redE{6}\times(\purpleE{2}\times\maroonE{4})\\\\
&=\redE{6}\times\goldE{8}\\\\ 
&=48
\end{align}$

Here are the completed equations:

$\begin{align}
\redE{6} \times \purpleE{2} \times \maroonE{4} &= \redE{6}\times(\purpleE{2}\times\maroonE{4})\\\\
&=\redE{6}\times\goldE{8}\\\\ 
&=48
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Atilio solved $\redE{3} \times \purpleE{5} \times\maroonE{2}$ using the equations below.

$\begin{align}
\redE{3} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2} &=(\redE{3}\times\purpleE{5}) \times\maroonE{2}\\\\ 
&=15 \times\maroonE{2}\\\\\  
&=30
\end{align}$

**Use the equations below to solve $\redE{3} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2}$ in a different way.**

||
-:|:-
$\redE{3} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2}$ | $=\redE{3}\times(\purpleE{5} \times {}$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $)$  |
|$= \redE{3}\times {}$  [[☃ numeric-input 2]]|
|$= 30$|
hints: The parentheses tell us which two numbers to multiply first.

Atilio solved the equation by multiplying $(\redE{3}\times\purpleE{5})$ first, and then multiplying by $\maroonE{2}$.

$\begin{align}
\redE{3} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2} &=(\redE{3}\times\purpleE{5}) \times\maroonE{2}\\\\
&=\blueE{15}\times\maroonE{2}\\\\
&=30
\end{align}$

In the new equation below, we want to multiply $(\purpleE{5}\times\maroonE{2})$ first, and then multiply by $\redE{3}$.

$\begin{align}
\redE{3} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2} &= \redE{3}\times(\purpleE{5}\times\maroonE{2})\\\\
&=\redE{3}\times\goldE{10}\\\\ 
&=30
\end{align}$

Here are the completed equations:

$\begin{align}
\redE{3} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{2} &= \redE{3}\times(\purpleE{5}\times\maroonE{2})\\\\
&=\redE{3}\times\goldE{10}\\\\ 
&=30
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Imani solved $\redE{2} \times \purpleE{3} \times\maroonE{7}$ using the equations below.

$\begin{align}
\redE{2} \times \purpleE{3} \times \maroonE{7} &=(\redE{2}\times\purpleE{3}) \times\maroonE{7}\\\\ 
&=6 \times\maroonE{7}\\\\\  
&=42
\end{align}$

**Use the equations below to solve $\redE{2} \times \purpleE{3} \times \maroonE{7}$ in a different way.**

||
-:|:-
$\redE{2} \times \purpleE{3} \times \maroonE{7}$ | $=\redE{2}\times(\purpleE{3} \times {}$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] $)$  |
|$= \redE{2}\times {}$  [[☃ numeric-input 2]]|
|$= 42$|
hints: The parentheses tell us which two numbers to multiply first.

Imani solved the equation by multiplying $(\redE{2}\times\purpleE{3})$ first, and then multiplying by $\maroonE{7}$.

$\begin{align}
\redE{2} \times \purpleE{3} \times \maroonE{7} &=(\redE{2}\times\purpleE{3}) \times\maroonE{7}\\\\
&=\blueE{6}\times\maroonE{7}\\\\
&=42
\end{align}$

In the new equation below, we want to multiply $(\purpleE{3}\times\maroonE{7})$ first, and then multiply by $\redE{2}$.

$\begin{align}
\redE{2} \times \purpleE{3} \times \maroonE{7} &= \redE{2}\times(\purpleE{3}\times\maroonE{7})\\\\
&=\redE{2}\times\goldE{21}\\\\ 
&=42
\end{align}$

Here are the completed equations:

$\begin{align}
\redE{2} \times \purpleE{3} \times \maroonE{7} &= \redE{2}\times(\purpleE{3}\times\maroonE{7})\\\\
&=\redE{2}\times\goldE{21}\\\\ 
&=42
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Ashanti solved $\redE{2} \times \purpleE{5} \times\maroonE{4}$ using the equations below.

$\begin{align}
\redE{2} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{4} &=\redE{2}\times(\purpleE{5} \times\maroonE{4})\\\\ 
&=\redE{2}\times 20 \\\\\  
&=40
\end{align}$

**Use the equations below to solve $\redE{2} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{4}$ in a different way.**

||
-:|:-
$\redE{2} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{4}$ | $=(\redE{2}\times {}$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]$)\times\maroonE{4}$  |
|$=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] ${}\times\maroonE{4}$|
|$= 40$|
hints: The parentheses tell us which two numbers to multiply first.

Ashanti solved the equation by multiplying $(\purpleE{5}\times\maroonE{4})$ first, and then multiplying by $\redE{2}$.

$\begin{align}
\redE{2} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{4} &= \redE{2}\times(\purpleE{5}\times\maroonE{4})\\\\
&=\redE{2}\times\goldE{20}\\\\ 
&=40
\end{align}$

In the new equation below, we want to multiply $(\redE{2}\times\purpleE{5})$ first, and then multiply by $\maroonE{4}$.

$\begin{align}
\redE{2} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{4} &=(\redE{2}\times\purpleE{5}) \times\maroonE{4}\\\\
&=\blueE{10}\times\maroonE{4}\\\\
&=40
\end{align}$

Here are the completed equations:

$\begin{align}
\redE{2} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{4} &=(\redE{2}\times\purpleE{5}) \times\maroonE{4}\\\\
&=\blueE{10}\times\maroonE{4}\\\\
&=40
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Rashawn solved $\redE{4} \times \purpleE{2} \times\maroonE{5}$ using the equations below.

$\begin{align}
\redE{4} \times \purpleE{2} \times \maroonE{5} &=\redE{4}\times(\purpleE{2} \times\maroonE{5})\\\\ 
&=\redE{4}\times 10 \\\\\  
&=40
\end{align}$

**Use the equations below to solve $\redE{4} \times \purpleE{2} \times \maroonE{5}$ in a different way.**

||
-:|:-
$\redE{4} \times \purpleE{2} \times \maroonE{5}$ | $=(\redE{4}\times {}$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]$)\times\maroonE{5}$  |
|$=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] ${}\times\maroonE{5}$|
|$= 40$|
hints: The parentheses tell us which two numbers to multiply first.

Rashawn solved the equation by multiplying $(\purpleE{2}\times\maroonE{5})$ first, and then multiplying by $\redE{4}$.

$\begin{align}
\redE{4} \times \purpleE{2} \times \maroonE{5} &= \redE{4}\times(\purpleE{2}\times\maroonE{5})\\\\
&=\redE{4}\times\goldE{10}\\\\ 
&=40
\end{align}$

In the new equation below, we want to multiply $(\redE{4}\times\purpleE{2})$ first, and then multiply by $\maroonE{5}$.

$\begin{align}
\redE{4} \times \purpleE{2} \times \maroonE{5} &=(\redE{4}\times\purpleE{2}) \times\maroonE{5}\\\\
&=\blueE{8}\times\maroonE{5}\\\\
&=40
\end{align}$

Here are the completed equations:

$\begin{align}
\redE{4} \times \purpleE{2} \times \maroonE{5} &=(\redE{4}\times\purpleE{2}) \times\maroonE{5}\\\\
&=\blueE{8}\times\maroonE{5}\\\\
&=40
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Issa solved $\redE{5} \times \purpleE{5} \times\maroonE{4}$ using the equations below.

\redE{5} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{4} &=\redE{5}\times(\purpleE{5} \times\maroonE{4})\\\\ 
&=\redE{5}\times 20 \\\\\  
&=100

**Use the equations below to solve $\redE{5} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{4}$ in a different way.**

||
-:|:-
$\redE{5} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{4}$ | $=($ [[☃ numeric-input 1]] ${}\times \purpleE{5})\times\maroonE{4}$  |
|$=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]] ${}\times\maroonE{4}$|
|$= 100$|
hints: The parentheses tell us which two numbers to multiply first.

Issa solved the equation by multiplying $(\purpleE{5}\times\maroonE{4})$ first, and then multiplying by $\redE{5}$.

$\begin{align}
\redE{5} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{4} &= \redE{5}\times(\purpleE{5}\times\maroonE{4})\\\\
&=\redE{5}\times\goldE{20}\\\\ 
&=100
\end{align}$

In the new equation below, we want to multiply $(\redE{5}\times\purpleE{5})$ first, and then multiply by $\maroonE{4}$.

$\begin{align}
\redE{5} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{4} &=(\redE{5}\times\purpleE{5}) \times\maroonE{4}\\\\
&=\blueE{25}\times\maroonE{4}\\\\
&=100
\end{align}$

Here are the completed equations:

$\begin{align}
\redE{5} \times \purpleE{5} \times \maroonE{4} &=(\redE{5}\times\purpleE{5}) \times\maroonE{4}\\\\
&=\blueE{25}\times\maroonE{4}\\\\
&=100
\end{align}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the products.**

$(2 \times 3) \times 5 = ~$[[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$2 \times (3 \times 5) = ~$[[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints: The parentheses in each equation tell us which two numbers to multiply first.

Let's complete the first equation:

$\phantom{=}\greenD{(2 \times 3)} \times 5$

$=\greenD{6} \times 5$

$=30$

Let's complete the second equation:

$\phantom{=}2 \times \blueD{(3 \times 5)}$

$=2 \times \blueD{15}$

$=30$

Here are the correct answers:

$(2 \times 3) \times 5 = 30$

$2 \times (3 \times 5) = 30$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the products.**

$4 \times (2 \times 10) = ~$[[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$(4 \times 2) \times 10 = ~$[[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints: The parentheses in each equation tell us which two numbers to multiply first.

Let's complete the first equation:

$\phantom{=}4 \times \greenD{(2 \times 10)}$

$=4 \times \greenD{20}$

$=80$

Let's complete the second equation:

$\phantom{=}\blueD{(4 \times 2)} \times 10$

$=\blueD8 \times 10$

$=80$

Here are the correct answers:

$4 \times (2 \times 10) =80$

$(4 \times 2) \times 10 =80$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the products.**

$5 \times (2 \times 3) = ~$[[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$(5 \times 2) \times 3 = ~$[[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints: The parentheses in each equation tell us which two numbers to multiply first.

Let's complete the first equation:

$\phantom{=}5 \times \greenD{(2 \times 3)}$

$=5 \times \greenD6$

$=30$

Let's complete the second equation:

$\phantom{=}\blueD{(5 \times 2)} \times 3$

$=\blueD{10} \times 3$

$=30$

Here are the correct answers:

$5 \times (2 \times 3) =30$

$(5 \times 2) \times 3 =30$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the products.**

$9 \times 3 \times 2 = ~$[[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$9 \times (3 \times 2) = ~$[[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
Let's complete the first equation. We can multiply the numbers in order:

$\phantom{=}\greenD{9 \times 3} \times 2$

$=\greenD{27} \times 2$

$=27 + 27$

$=54$

However, it is probably easier to multiply the last two numbers first:

$\phantom{=}9 \times \blueD{3 \times 2}$

$=9 \times \blueD6$

$=54$

Let's complete the second equation. The parentheses tell us which two numbers to multiply first.

$\phantom{=}9 \times \blueD{(3 \times 2)}$

$=9 \times \blueD6$

$=54$

Here are the correct answers:

$9 \times 3 \times 2 =54$

$9 \times (3 \times 2) =54$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the products.**

$7 \times 2 \times 3 = ~$[[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$7 \times (2 \times 3) = ~$[[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
Let's complete the first equation. We can multiply the numbers in order:

$\phantom{=}\greenD{7 \times 2} \times 3$

$=\greenD{14} \times 3$

$=14 + 14 + 14$

$=42$

However, it is probably easier to multiply the last two numbers first:

$\phantom{=}7 \times \blueD{2 \times 3}$

$=7 \times \blueD6$

$=42$

Let's complete the second equation. The parentheses tell us which two numbers to multiply first.

$\phantom{=}7 \times \blueD{(2 \times 3)}$

$=7 \times \blueD6$

$=42$

Here are the correct answers:

$7 \times 2 \times 3 =42$

$7 \times (2 \times 3) =42$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the products.**

$9 \times (2 \times 4) = ~$[[☃ numeric-input 2]]

$9 \times 2 \times 4 = ~$[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
Let's complete the first equation. The parentheses tell us which two numbers to multiply first.

$\phantom{=}9 \times \blueD{(2 \times 4)}$

$=9 \times \blueD8$

$=72$

Let's complete the second equation. We can multiply the numbers in order:

$\phantom{=}\greenD{9 \times 2} \times 4$

$=\greenD{18} \times 4$

$=18 + 18 + 18 + 18$

$=72$

However, it is probably easier to multiply the last two numbers first:

$\phantom{=}9 \times \blueD{2 \times 4}$

$=9 \times \blueD8$

$=72$

Here are the correct answers:

$9 \times (2 \times 4) =72$

$9 \times 2 \times 4 =72$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Find the products.**

$8 \times (5 \times 2) = ~$[[☃ numeric-input 1]]

$(8 \times 5) \times 2 = ~$[[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints: The parentheses in each equation tell us which two numbers to multiply first.

Let's complete the first equation:

$\phantom{=}8 \times \greenD{(5 \times 2)}$

$=8 \times \greenD{10}$

$=80$

Let's complete the second equation:

$\phantom{=}\blueD{(8 \times 5)} \times 2$

$=\blueD{40} \times 2$

$=80$

Here are the correct answers:

$8 \times (5 \times 2) =80$

$(8 \times 5) \times 2 =80$

assessment_items.question_content:
Sam tried to evaluate an expression step by step.

$\qquad\begin{align}
&9 \times 5 \times 2\\\\
\\
=&9 \times (5 \times 2)&\greenD{\text{Step } 1}\\\\
\\
=&9 \times 10&\blueD{\text{Step } 2}\\\\
\\
=&90&\purpleC{\text{Step } 3} \\\\
\end{align}$

**Find Sam's mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\greenD{\text{Step }1}$ correctly put parentheses around $5 \times 2$.

The parentheses helped Sam figure out what two numbers to multiply first.
$\blueD{\text{Step }2}$ correctly multiplied $5 \times 2$ to get  $10$.
$\purpleC{\text{Step }3}$ correctly multiplied $9 \times 10$ to get $90$.
Sam did not make a mistake.

choices.content: Sam did not make a mistake.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ram tried to evaluate an expression step by step.

$\qquad\begin{align}
&6 \times 3 \times 2\\\\
\\
=&6 \times (3 \times 2)&\greenD{\text{Step } 1}\\\\
\\
=&6 \times 6&\blueD{\text{Step } 2}\\\\
\\
=&36&\purpleC{\text{Step } 3} \\\\
\end{align}$

**Find Ram's mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\greenD{\text{Step }1}$ correctly put parentheses around $3 \times 2$.

The parentheses helped  Ram figure out what two numbers to multiply first.
$\blueD{\text{Step }2}$ correctly multiplied $3 \times 2$ to get  $6$.
$\purpleC{\text{Step }3}$ correctly multiplied $6 \times 6$ to get $36$.
Ram did not make a mistake.

choices.content: Ram did not make a mistake.

assessment_items.question_content:
Erin tried to evaluate an expression step by step.

$\qquad\begin{align}
&8 \times 2 \times 5\\\\
\\
=&8 \times (2 \times 5)&\greenD{\text{Step } 1}\\\\
\\
=&16 \times 10&\blueD{\text{Step } 2}\\\\
\\
=&160&\purpleC{\text{Step } 3} \\\\
\end{align}$

**Find Erin's mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\greenD{\text{Step }1}$ correctly put parentheses around $2 \times 5$.

The parentheses helped Erin figure out what two numbers to multiply first.

$\blueD{\text{Step }2}$  correctly multiplied $2 \times 5$ to get  $10$.

However, $8$ was changed to $16$. This is a mistake!
Erin's mistake was in $\blueD{\text{Step }2}$.

choices.content: Erin did not make a mistake.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kim tried to evaluate an expression step by step.

$\qquad\begin{align}
&5 \times 2 \times 4\\\\
\\
=&(5 \times 2) \times 4&\greenD{\text{Step } 1}\\\\
\\
=&10 + 4&\blueD{\text{Step } 2}\\\\
\\
=&14&\purpleC{\text{Step } 3} \\\\
\end{align}$

**Find Kim's mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\greenD{\text{Step }1}$ correctly put parentheses around $5 \times 2$.

The parentheses helped Kim figure out what two numbers to multiply first.

$\blueD{\text{Step }2}$  correctly multiplied $5 \times 2$ to get  $10$.

However, $\times4$ was changed to $+4$. This is a mistake!
Kim's mistake was in $\blueD{\text{Step }2}$.

choices.content: Kim did not make a mistake.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kat tried to evaluate an expression step by step.

$\qquad\begin{align}
&7 \times 5 \times 2\\\\
\\
=&7 \times (5 \times 2)&\greenD{\text{Step } 1}\\\\
\\
=&7 \times 10&\blueD{\text{Step } 2}\\\\
\\
=&17&\purpleC{\text{Step } 3} \\\\
\end{align}$

**Find Kat's mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\greenD{\text{Step }1}$ correctly put parentheses around $5 \times 2$.

The parentheses helped Kat figure out what two numbers to multiply first.
$\blueD{\text{Step }2}$  correctly multiplied $5 \times 2$ to get  $10$.

$\purpleC{\text{Step }3}$  multiplied $7 \times 10$ to get  $17$. 

This is a mistake! $7 \times 10 = 70.$
Kat's mistake was in $\purpleC{\text{Step }3}$.

choices.content: Kat did not make a mistake.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ty tried to evaluate an expression step by step.

$\qquad\begin{align}
&8 \times 2 \times 3\\\\
\\
=&8 \times (2 \times 3)&\greenD{\text{Step } 1}\\\\
\\
=&8 \times 6&\blueD{\text{Step } 2}\\\\
\\
=&42&\purpleC{\text{Step } 3} \\\\
\end{align}$

**Find Ty's mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\greenD{\text{Step }1}$ correctly put parentheses around $2 \times 3$.

The parentheses helped Ty figure out what two numbers to multiply first.
$\blueD{\text{Step }2}$  correctly multiplied $2 \times 3$ to get  $6$.

$\purpleC{\text{Step }3}$  multiplied $8 \times 6$ to get  $42$. 

This is a mistake! $8 \times 6 = 48.$
Ty's mistake was in $\purpleC{\text{Step }3}$.

choices.content: Ty did not make a mistake.

assessment_items.question_content:
Bo tried to evaluate an expression step-by-step.

$\begin{align}
&6 \times 5 \times 3\\\\
\\
=&(6 \times 5) \times 3&\greenD{\text{Step } 1}\\\\
\\
=&30 + 3&\blueD{\text{Step } 2}\\\\
\\
=&33&\purpleC{\text{Step } 3} \\\\
\end{align}$

**Find Bo's mistake.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\greenD{\text{Step }1}$ correctly put parentheses around $6\times 5$.

The parentheses helped Bo figure out what two numbers to multiply first.

$\blueD{\text{Step }2}$  correctly multiplied $6 \times 5$ to get  $30$.

However, ${}\times3$ was changed to ${}+3$. This is a mistake!
Bo's mistake was in $\blueD{\text{Step }2}$.

choices.content: Bo did not make a mistake.
